title: Module 11: Giving Information, Advice & Feedback
learning_objective: Ask permission before sharing information or advice; Build on what the person already knows; Keep information brief and neutral; Invite patient's interpretation
scenario_context: Learn to share information collaboratively using the Elicit-Provide-Elicit approach

persona_config.name: What
persona_config.background: Learn to share information collaboratively using the Elicit-Provide-Elicit approach

info_1.persona_text: I've been trying to lose weight, but I'm not sure I really understand what I need to do differently. What should I be doing?

choice_points.option_text: You need to create a calorie deficit. That means eating less and exercising more. The evidence clearly shows that's the only way to lose weight.

feedback.immediate: This jumps straight to telling without first exploring what patient knows or asking permission.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: I'd be happy to share some information about that. First, what do you already know about weight loss - what have you heard or read?

feedback.immediate: Perfect - accepts the request and starts by eliciting what they already know.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: It's really about willpower and discipline. If you're not losing weight, you're not trying hard enough.

feedback.immediate: This provides judgmental feedback that will likely create defensiveness.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

info_2a.persona_text: I know all that. It's not like I'm stupid. I just can't seem to make it work.

choice_points.option_text: I didn't say you were stupid. I'm just trying to help. You asked me what to do.

feedback.immediate: This becomes argumentative and damages the relationship further.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You're right, I jumped in too quickly. You've already been working on this. What's your experience been so far with trying to lose weight?

feedback.immediate: Good recovery - practitioner takes responsibility and pivots to eliciting.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

info_2b.persona_text: Well, I know I need to eat less and move more. I've tried counting calories before. It worked for a while but then I got tired of tracking everything.

choice_points.option_text: Right, so you understand the basics and have experience with calorie tracking. Would it be okay if I shared some information about different approaches that might fit better with your lifestyle?

feedback.immediate: Excellent - acknowledges what they know and explicitly asks permission before providing information.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Well, you need to keep tracking. Most people who stop tracking regain the weight. That's just how it works.

feedback.immediate: This gives directive advice without permission or exploring alternatives.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You're obviously not seeing the bigger picture. Let me explain the science to you.

feedback.immediate: This belittles the patient and takes an expert-superior position.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

info_3a.persona_text: Sure, I'm open to hearing about different approaches.

choice_points.option_text: One thing some people find helpful is focusing on food quality rather than just quantity - like emphasizing whole foods, vegetables, lean proteins. Others find success with pattern changes like eating at regular times or reducing portions gradually rather than tracking every calorie. Some combine small changes in eating with increased activity. Different approaches work for different people.

feedback.immediate: Good - provides information neutrally, briefly, and presents options rather than directives.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Well, the research shows that calorie tracking is the most effective method. People who track consistently lose the most weight and keep it off. You really should give it another try but this time stick with it. Here are all the apps I recommend, and let me explain how to calculate your basal metabolic rate...

feedback.immediate: This provides too much information, is overly prescriptive, and doesn't check understanding.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

info_4a.persona_text: That's interesting. I hadn't thought about the food quality approach instead of just counting.

choice_points.option_text: What do you make of that idea? How does that land with you?

feedback.immediate: Perfect - invites the patient's interpretation and returns control to them.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Yes, food quality is definitely the best approach. That's what you should focus on.

feedback.immediate: This tells the patient what to think rather than eliciting their perspective.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: There's a lot more I need to tell you about nutrition science. Let me explain macros and micronutrients...

feedback.immediate: This dumps more information without checking how the first information was received.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

info_5a.persona_text: It sounds more doable than tracking everything. Like, I could focus on adding more vegetables and choosing grilled chicken instead of fried, that kind of thing?

choice_points.option_text: You're already thinking about specific, practical ways you could apply that approach. What appeals to you about that way of thinking about food?

feedback.immediate: Excellent - affirms patient's application and continues exploring their perspective.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Well, you'd also need to watch portions, avoid processed foods, eliminate added sugars, increase fiber...

feedback.immediate: This loads on more requirements instead of staying with the patient's understanding.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

info_end_good.persona_text: I think it's less overwhelming. With tracking, I felt like I was obsessing over every calorie. This feels more like I could just make better choices without the constant math. I think I could try that.

info_end_poor.persona_text: I feel more confused now than when I started. Maybe I should just forget about it.
